description: HTTP services usually expose websites, APIs, admin panels, files, directories, or web applications.
why_prioritize: Web services often expose login pages, hidden directories, CMS platforms, APIs, and version leaks.
In CTFs and labs, HTTP is commonly one of the first services worth checking manually.

enumeration_steps.title: Open the website in a browser
enumeration_steps.reason: The browser view gives quick context about what the service is hosting.

enumeration_steps.title: Check headers and technologies
enumeration_steps.reason: Headers and technology detection can reveal servers, frameworks, CMS names, and version leaks.

enumeration_steps.title: Check common discovery files
enumeration_steps.reason: Files like robots.txt and sitemap.xml can reveal hidden paths or areas the site owner did not want indexed.

enumeration_steps.title: Run directory discovery
enumeration_steps.reason: Hidden directories and files often reveal admin panels, backups, uploads, or configuration files.

next_steps: Try common or default credentials if the application looks like a known product.
Check for username enumeration through login errors, password reset pages, or account lookup behavior.
Review the page source for comments, hidden fields, test users, or old development notes.
If you find valid credentials later, return to this page and test the authenticated area.
Test whether your input is reflected back on the page.
Check for basic XSS behavior using safe lab payloads.
Look for server-side errors that reveal framework names, file paths, or backend behavior.
Capture the request in Burp Suite and check all parameters, not only the visible form fields.
Check which file extensions are allowed.
Test whether the application validates content type, file extension, or both.
Find where uploaded files are stored and whether they can be accessed directly.
Look for weak filtering, renamed files, or image processing behavior.
Review file names for backups, notes, credentials, source code, or old archives.
Download interesting files for offline review.
Check whether hidden files or nested directories are exposed.
Take note of dates and naming patterns because they can reveal older versions or forgotten paths.
Visit each discovered path manually.
Use the paths as seeds for more directory discovery.
Look for admin panels, backup folders, staging areas, or old application routes.
Do not assume disallowed paths are sensitive, but treat them as useful leads.
Search for known issues affecting that exact product and version.
Check whether default admin paths exist.
Look for public documentation to understand the normal file structure.
Use version details as a lead, then verify manually before assuming it is vulnerable.
Read the file carefully for credentials, database names, internal paths, or comments.
Check whether similar backup extensions exist, such as .bak, .old, .zip, .tar.gz, or ~.
Search inside downloaded files for passwords, tokens, usernames, and hostnames.
Use any discovered paths or credentials to guide the next enumeration step.

beginner_notes: Do not rely only on automated tools. Open the page manually too.
Check the page source when the page looks empty.
If the port is 443 or the service is HTTPS, use https:// instead of http://.
Take notes of interesting paths and test them again after finding credentials.
